rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 19, 2024, line 1, 1 - PLAINTIFF GIANFRANCO MURARI, TRUSTEE OF THE FREDERICKS-MURARI FAMILY TRUST UNDER DECLARATION OF TRUST DATED MARCH 19, 2012 AND EXECUTOR AND PERSONAL REPRESENTATIVE OF THE ESTATE OF ROBERT FREDERICKS, DECEASED MOTION FOR TERMINATING SANCTIONS OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE FURTHER SANCTIONS (TENATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2) Defendants also cite authority for the proposition that claims against the individual defendants must be supported by admissible evidence which is of scant supply in this case given the dearth of discovery to date. See Baragan v. Banco BCH, 188 Cal.App.3d 283, 302 (1986). As for plaintiff's assertion in the proposed default judgment that the damages to be awarded to the individual defendants are supported in part by elder abuse allegations in the First Amended Complaint, that claim was dismissed with prejudice by the Court on November 3, 2023. To the extent plaintiff's direct claims against the individual defendants rely upon his breach of contract claims arising from the alleged failure to pay the estate the value of the decedent's 50% shareholding in RISE, the plaintiff's own allegations in paragraphs 16 and 109 of the First Amended Complaint establish that the value of those shares must be set by a third party appraisal, which the Court does not believe has yet been conducted. Plaintiff adds that damage claims on behalf of RISE are defective because the decedent dissolved it years ago, rendering it unable to conduct business, perform contracts or maintain contract interference claims such as those alleged by plaintiff. It is clear on this record that procedurally and substantively, no default judgment can be entered at this time - with the exception of the award of $600 under the Nineteenth Cause of Action. Assuming the entry of terminating sanctions is ordered, plaintiff is going to need to schedule an evidentiary hearing, conduct substantial discovery and/or engage an appraiser. For the above reasons, the Court declines to enter a default judgment at this time, at least a judgment similar to the one proposed by plaintiff, save and except for an award of $600. But that award should await the entry of a one final judgment rather than piecemeal awards. For the 9:00 a.m. Discovery calendar, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link (DISCOVERY, DEPARTMENT 302 DAILY AT 9:00 A.M.), or dial the corresponding number and use the meeting ID, and password for Discovery Department 302. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: GIANFRANCO MURARI VS. ELVIA ALEMAN-MARTINEZ ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Terminating Sanctions Or In The Alternative Further Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 19, 2024, line 1, 1 - PLAINTIFF GIANFRANCO MURARI, TRUSTEE OF THE FREDERICKS-MURARI FAMILY TRUST UNDER DECLARATION OF TRUST DATED MARCH 19, 2012 AND EXECUTOR AND PERSONAL REPRESENTATIVE OF THE ESTATE OF ROBERT FREDERICKS, DECEASED Motion For Terminating Sanctions Or In The Alternative Further Sanctions (tenativer ruling part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge Philip Ward, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: As the Court noted in an earlier tentative ruling, this is a derivative action alleging breach of various fiduciary duties by defendants premised upon defendants' management and operation of two educational institutions: RISE, Inc. and ARISE, Inc. Plaintiff alleges his status as trustee of the Fredericks- Murari Family trust and representative of the deceased Robert Fredericks, a co-founder, 50% shareholder and director of RISE, Inc. Plaintiff seeks damages, declaratory relief and a variety of equitable forms of relief. At a hearing on April 25, 2024, the Court issued a tentative ruling granting plaintiff's motion for terminating sanctions and entry of a default judgment. This tentative ruling was based upon defendants' virtual abandonment of their responsibilities as defendants to comply with court orders and provide discovery on a timely basis. Since defendants did not stipulate to the Judge Pro Tem handling this matter being authorized to resolve the merits of the pending motion, after the April 25 hearing, the JPT sent a report to Judge Ulmer recommending that the Court adopt the tentative ruling previously issued. When the matter was called before Judge Ulmer on May 15, 2024, the Court remanded the matter to the JPT for clarification concerning the form of a proposed default judgment and the relief to which plaintiff would be entitled thereunder. The remanded matter was set for rehearing before the JPT on July 19, 2024. In the interim, counsel for plaintiff had prepared a proposed default judgment containing extensive relief to which plaintiff was purportedly due from defendants. Included in that judgment were awards of damages of $623,000 (rounded) for plaintiff's derivative claims on behalf of RISE Institute, Inc., and $245,000 from the individual defendants on plaintiff's "direct claims". The proposed form of judgment also included entry of various kinds of equitable relief including imposition of a constructive trust, removing certain defendants from positions with RISE and enjoining named defendants from competing with RISE. The smallest and most specific award in the proposed judgment was $600.00 as against defendant Aleman-Martinez under the Nineteenth Cause of Action of plaintiff's First Amended Complaint. [end of tentative ruling part 1 see part 2] = (302/JPT)

rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Friday, July 19, 2024, Line 2. 2 - DEFENDANT SAN FRANCISCO ZEN CENTER, A NON-PROFIT ORGANIZATION, LINDA A. GALIJAN, AN INDIVIDUAL AND MIKE SMITH, AN INDIVIDUAL's Motion To File Under Seal. Defendants' motion to seal is denied without prejudice. To enable a court to make the specific findings required by California Rule of Court 2.550(d), a party moving to seal court records must present "specific enumeration of the facts sought to be withheld and specific reasons for withholding them." (See H.B. Fuller Co. v. Doe (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 879, 894.) Here, while exhibits are listed, the specific facts sought to be withheld are not enumerated. Moreover, while broad reasons for withholding documents (e.g., "sensitive, confidential") are also listed, little or no attempt is made to link facts with reasons. Plaintiff's motion for leave to file First Amended Complaint is granted. There is a liberal policy in favor of amendment. "If the motion to amend is timely made and the granting of the motion will not prejudice the opposing party, it is error to refuse permission to amend." (Morgan v. Sup.Ct. rulings.Case Title: SAMEER BHADOURIA ET AL VS. MSASA PROPERITES, LLC, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT MSASA PROPERTIES, LLC'S MOTION FOR PROTECTIVE ORDER REGARDING PROPERTIES OWNED PROPERTIES BOUGHT AND SOLD AND PROPERTIES OWNED AND RENOVATED REQUEST FOR SANCTIONS (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 19, 2024, line 3, DEFENDANT MSASA PROPERTIES, LLC'S MOTION FOR PROTECTIVE ORDER REGARDING PROPERTIES OWNED PROPERTIES BOUGHT AND SOLD AND PROPERTIES OWNED AND RENOVATED REQUEST FOR SANCTIONS (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 4) In support of its motion for a protective order, defendant argues that the three special interrogatories are not relevant to the subject matter of this action, they invade defendant's privacy interests (specifically its interest in financial privacy) and they are objectionably vague and overbroad. Plaintiffs dispute the validity and applicability of each and every ground upon which defendant's motion for protective order rests. As both plaintiffs and defendant recognize, California rules governing pretrial discovery are interpreted liberally and expansively. In the memorandum of points and authorities in support of their second motion to compel referenced above (at p. 4), plaintiffs succinctly summarize those rules: "The scope of discovery is quite broad permitting discovery regarding any matter that is relevant to the subject matter. (CCP 2017.010.) The 'subject matter' is broader than issues framed in the pleadings and includes potential issues in the case. (Pacific Tel. & Tel. Co. v. Superior Court, 2 C3d 161, 172-173, 84 CR 718, 465 P2d 854 (1970).) For purposes of discovery, information is considered relevant if it might reasonably assist a party in evaluating the case, preparing for trial, or facilitating settlement thereof. (Lipton v. Superior Court, 48 Cal.App.4th 1599, 1611 (1996); Norton v. Superior Court (1994) 24 CA4th, 1750, 1760.) The party seeking discovery is entitled to substantial leeway and doubts are resolved in favor of allowing discovery. (Norton, at 1761.)." (end of part 2 see part 3 tentative ruling) = (302/JPT)

rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 19, 2024, line 3, DEFENDANT MSASA PROPERTIES, LLC'S MOTION FOR PROTECTIVE ORDER REGARDING PROPERTIES OWNED PROPERTIES BOUGHT AND SOLD AND PROPERTIES OWNED AND RENOVATED REQUEST FOR SANCTIONS (TENTATIVE RULING PART 4 OF 4) Defendant's contention that its financial privacy would be compromised if compelled to provide the information sought by plaintiffs is simply a bridge too far. No acquisition or sale information is requested, and no revenue or overhead numbers from which profits might be gleaned is sought nor need it be provided. All defendant is asked to provide is addresses and dates. If later discovery results in potential exposure of sensitive financial information, that is the time for defendant to seek a protective order - one that limits the use and dissemination of the data but not one that bars its discovery in the first place. Accordingly, for good cause shown (or perhaps the lack of same), defendant's motion for a protective order and an award of sanctions is DENIED. rulings.Case Title: SAMEER BHADOURIA ET AL VS. MSASA PROPERITES, LLC, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT MSASA PROPERTIES, LLC'S MOTION FOR PROTECTIVE ORDER REGARDING PROPERTIES OWNED PROPERTIES BOUGHT AND SOLD AND PROPERTIES OWNED AND RENOVATED REQUEST FOR SANCTIONS (TENTATIVE RULING PART 3 OF 3)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 19, 2024, line 3, DEFENDANT MSASA PROPERTIES, LLC'S MOTION FOR PROTECTIVE ORDER REGARDING PROPERTIES OWNED PROPERTIES BOUGHT AND SOLD AND PROPERTIES OWNED AND RENOVATED REQUEST FOR SANCTIONS (TENTATIVE RULING PART 3 OF 4) In support of the three special interrogatories, plaintiffs allege that defendant is and has acted as a "developer" of properties which it then renovates/remodels for purposes of (hopefully profitable) resale, a business model colloquially referred to as "property flipping" - a real estate investing tactic that involves buying a home, fixing it up and reselling it quickly. Although defendant has denied this allegation, for purposes of pretrial discovery plaintiffs have no obligation to prove it up or support it with a good cause showing. Williams v. Superior Court, 3 Cal.5th 531, 550 (2017). The evident purpose of the three interrogatories at issue is to determine just how often over time defendant has purchased properties in need of renovation, arranged/contracted for such renovations and then resold the properties to willing third parties such as Ms. Ongpin, the purchaser from whom plaintiffs bought the Alvarado Street home. The sought-after information would purportedly be used to establish customs and practices employed by defendant in so conducting its business, including for example how often (and close) defendant has worked with defendant AMC Builders, Inc., the contractor hired by defendant to perform the allegedly bungled and defective renovations and to which defendant not so subtly points in its summary judgment motion. The requested information could well show whether and how often defendant has been accused of below-standard renovations in the past and the relationships (if any) defendant and AMC Builders have developed with the San Francisco Department of Building Inspection, the municipal agency issuing the permits and conducting the inspections upon which defendant relies heavily in its summary judgment motion (see, e.g., Mohammed Nuru, Inspector Bernard Curren). All of this information would potentially lead to admissible evidence as to the standard of care defendant should be subject to as an allegedly experienced and knowledgeable residential developer/renovator. In sum, the Court finds that the three special interrogatories are indeed relevant to the subject matter of this action and reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of evidence admissible at trial. Nor are they vague and overbroad. As for defendant's financial privacy interests, both sides cite to the dispositive holding in SCC Acquisitions, Inc. v. Superior Court, 243 Cal.App.4th 741, 755-756 (2015) for the principle that companies have limited privacy interests that need to be balanced against whether the sought-after information is reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. Here, as outlined above, there are certainly reasonable and relevant purposes for which the identification of properties called for in the three interrogatories (by address, when purchased and when sold) would be useful. As such, this information could well lead to admissible evidence at trial. (end part 3, see part 4 tentative ruling) = (302/JPT)

The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Onward Partners ("plaintiff" or "cross-defendant") is an advertising and brand strategy company based in San Francisco. For the last few years, its shares have been held equally by two individuals: Jeffrey Loomis and Quentin Cotillard. Loomis operated as the CEO of the company while Cotillard filled the position of Chief Financial Officer and head of technological operations. This suit was initiated (evidently with Loomis' approval) by plaintiff against Cotillard after the latter had resigned his positions with the company. Plaintiff accused Cotillard of a variety of tortious activities including breach of fiduciary duty, negligence and commercial defamation. When served with the plaintiff's complaint, Cotillard filed a cross-complaint against Loomis and cross-defendant in which Cotillard accused Loomis of not attending to business, diverting company revenues, misrepresentations and outright fraud, among other things. To support his cross-complaint, Cotillard served form interrogatories on cross-defendants which appear to have been responded to individually and on behalf of the company by Loomis. Dissatisfied with several amended responses to form interrogatory No. 15.1, and after meeting and conferring with first plaintiff's counsel and then with Mr. Loomis (who is now appearing in propria persona), Cotillard has filed a motion for an order compelling further responses to that single form interrogatory together with an award of sanctions from the cross-defendants. Form Interrogatory No. 15.1 provides as follows: Identify each denial of a material allegation and each special or affirmative defense in your pleadings and for each: (a) State all facts upon which you base the denial or special or affirmative defense; (b) State the names, ADDRESSES, and telephone numbers of all PERSONS who have knowledge of those facts; and (c) Identify all DOCUMENTS and other tangible things that support your denial or special or affirmative defense, and state the name, ADDRESS, and telephone number of the PERSON who has each DOCUMENT. rulings.Case Title: ONWARD PARTNERS, A NEVADA CORPORATION DBA ONWARD VS. QUENTIN COTILLARD, AN INDIVIDUAL ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT QUENTIN COTILLARD, AN INDIVIDUAL NOTICE OF MOTION AND MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSE TO FORM INTERROGATORY NO. 15.1 AND REQUEST FOR MONETARY SANCTIONS (PART 2 OF 4 TENATIVE RULING)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 19, 2024, line 4, DEFENDANT QUENTIN COTILLARD, AN INDIVIDUAL NOTICE OF MOTION AND MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSE TO FORM INTERROGATORY NO. 15.1 AND REQUEST FOR MONETARY SANCTIONS (PART 2 OF 3 TENATIVE RULING) As noted above, Loomis and/or plaintiff offered a number of amended responses to Interrogatory 15.1. Several responses included objections, none of which the Court deems supported when offered in response to a form interrogatory, never mind a more specific and focused interrogatory such as No. 15.1. The question is neither vague nor overbroad under the liberal rules governing discovery in California. To the degree Loomis' amended responses assert privilege as a ground for objection, Cotillard's motion expressly does not challenge or dispute those objections. In this case, No. 15.1 focuses on two distinct subjects: (1) a party's denials of the proponent's material allegations (such as in an answer to a complaint or cross-complaint) and (2) any affirmative defenses the party has offered in its pleadings (e.g., in that same answer). Addressing the first subject, Loomis and cross-defendant have provided no responses, factual or otherwise, in support of their denials of the allegations in Cotillard's cross-complaint. Accordingly, Cotillard's motion for an order compelling Loomis and cross-defendant to provide further responses to that first prong of No.15.1 is GRANTED. Loomis and cross-defendant shall have 15 days from the date of this Court's order to submit verified supplementary responses to No. 15.1 as provided herein. The second prong of No. 15.1 focuses on affirmative defenses. In his several amended responses, Loomis and cross-defendant inch forward, providing more information each time. The record before the Court reflects cumulative responses that address each affirmative defense and provide some further information about facts, documents and persons who can support some or all of those facts. The issue is their sufficiency. As for the responses about the affirmative defenses to Cotillard's cross-complaint, it appears Loomis/cross-complainant have responded as best they can. While it is true a party representing him or itself cannot use that as an excuse for not responding adequately to discovery, Loomis' attempts to support affirmative defenses such as laches, statute of limitations and the like appear to provide as much foundation as a layperson can be expected to offer. Often, affirmative defenses included in an answer are only fleshed out much later in the litigation. On the present record, the Court concludes that the challenged responses need not be further supplemented. (end of part 2 see part 3, tentative ruling) = (302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: OSCAR ANTONIO MENDEZ ESQUIVEL VS. AMERICAN HONDA MOTOR CO., INC., A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: PLAINTIFF OSCAR ANTONIO MENDEZ ESQUIVEL , AN INDIVIDUAL NOTICE OF MOTION AND MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO PLAINTIFF'S REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS, SET TWO (PART 2 OF 3)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 19, 2024, line 5, PLAINTIFF OSCAR ANTONIO MENDEZ ESQUIVEL , AN INDIVIDUAL NOTICE OF MOTION AND MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO PLAINTIFF'S REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS, SET TWO (PART 2 OF 5) The San Francisco Superior Court has before it a large number of Song-Beverly lawsuits, often brought by the same plaintiff firms and defended by the same defense law firms. The Court has had before it at least two of these suits in which further document productions were demanded by the plaintiff, whether Set 1 or Set 2. These actions have provided an education for the Court in these types of disputes and influenced its opinions. See Mitchell v. Volkswagen Group of America, Superior Court No. CGC 23-609331 and Parra-Orozco v. Volkswagen Group of America, Superior Court No. CGC 23-604692. In previous cases handled by the firm representing plaintiff Esquivel, the Court has been provided with general orders issued by the Los Angeles Superior Court in Song-Beverly litigation. Those orders, inter alia, include a scope of mandated discovery to include all vehicles sold by the defendant in California of the same year, make and model as the plaintiff's vehicle. The Court will, as it did in previous cases, adopt the same scope as in the Los Angeles court. No nationwide inquiries need be made, just in California. As a footnote, and for what it is worth, the Court notes that in an earlier discovery proceeding in this case dealing with Set 1 of plaintiff's production requests, the Court issued an order requiring further responses to Request No. 30 inquiring into other Honda vehicle defects (same year, make and model) without any geographical limitations. When it comes to the plaintiff's meet and confer obligations under the Discovery Act, it remains the case that plaintiff's letters to defense counsel are pro forma and themselves products of the same computer-generated cut-and-paste properties referenced above. That said, each side in this litigation has its positions staked out that no amount of meeting and conferring are going to change. Therefore, any meet and confer objections by defendant are overruled as essentially moot. (end of part 2 see part 3)

rulings.Case Title: OSCAR ANTONIO MENDEZ ESQUIVEL VS. AMERICAN HONDA MOTOR CO., INC., A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: PLAINTIFF OSCAR ANTONIO MENDEZ ESQUIVEL , AN INDIVIDUAL NOTICE OF MOTION AND MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO PLAINTIFF'S REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS, SET TWO (PART 3 OF 3)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 19, 2024, line 5, PLAINTIFF OSCAR ANTONIO MENDEZ ESQUIVEL , AN INDIVIDUAL NOTICE OF MOTION AND MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO PLAINTIFF'S REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS, SET TWO (PART 3 OF 5) Next, the Court addresses the fact that plaintiff seeks additional responses to all 32 requests regardless it defendant offered objection-only responses or, after its usual litany of objections often unmoored to the facts of the case, it responded that it had searched high and low and did not find any responsive documents. As to this second category, including Request Nos. 1, 5, 7, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 16, 22, 23, 24, 25, 27, 28, 29, 30 and 32, the motion to compel further responses is (unless noted below), DENIED. The Court does, however, find that in several responses defendant used a phrase that renders its response defective and objectionable: "and to which no objection is being made". See, e.g., Request No. 2. Each and every response by defendant that employs that phrase is deemed objectionable and deficient, warranting an order GRANTING plaintiff's motion compelling further direct and non-evasive responses. Similarly, when defendant conditions a willingness to provide documents on the entry of a protective order when it has not proposed or entered into such an order, that response is objectionable and deficient. Plaintiff's counsel represents it routinely offers defendants the protective order blessed by the Los Angeles Superior Court in Song-Beverly litigation. Counsel are directed to follow up on that subject. Any of defendant's responses which are so conditioned are overruled, and plaintiff's motion to compel is GRANTED. Request No. 3 is reasonably read to seek documents related to leaks into the trunk and leaks into the rear hatch as referenced in notices by Honda to dealers re the same year, model, etc. of Honda Civics. The motion to compel is GRANTED with this reading of the request. Request No. 4 is responded to with only boilerplate objections unsupported by facts as to privilege, confidentiality and the like. Recall policies and procedures could product relevant information for a Song-Beverly claim which would be admissible is within the scope recited above. The motion as to this request is GRANTED. Request No. 6 is very broad in the context of as large an organization as the defendant appears to be. But the defendant has offered to produce the documents, so the request is DENIED. The response to Request No. 8 appears to the Court to be reasonably responsive and direct. Motion to compel further response is DENIED. (end of part 3 of 5 tentative ruling) = (302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DANIELLE DI BARI VS. WOODLINE PARTNERS LP, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT WOODLINE PARTNERS LP MOTION TO COMPEL COMPLIANCE WITH THE COURTS FEBRUARY 2, 2024 DISCOVERY ORDER AND FOR SANCTIONS FOR FAILURE TO OBEY ORDER (PART 3 OF 5)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 19, 2024, line 6, DEFENDANT WOODLINE PARTNERS LP MOTION TO COMPEL COMPLIANCE WITH THE COURTS FEBRUARY 2, 2024 DISCOVERY ORDER AND FOR SANCTIONS FOR FAILURE TO OBEY ORDER (PART 3 OF 5) Plaintiff also offers a sort of umbrella objection that defendants cannot seek such multi-pronged relief in a single motion. Instead, citing to Rule 3.111(a)-(c) of the Rules of Court, plaintiff contends the defendants should have filed a separate motion for each form of relief being sought. Addressing this over-arching objection first, the Court finds no support in the Rules of Court or elsewhere for plaintiff's contention that the defendants' motion should be denied because there weren't six motions filed instead of just one. Some months ago, the Court was confronted with a case in which two plaintiffs each served four discovery requests upon each of five related defendants, resulting in 40 discovery demands. The plaintiffs, dissatisfied with the defendants' responses, then filed 40 individual motions addressing the asserted inadequacies in each of the discovery demands. The Court could not then, and cannot now, think of a more time-consuming, inefficient and inappropriate contention that a more or less singular discovery motion must be subdivided in such a fashion or risk denial by the already overburdened law and motion department of this Court. Returning to defendants' enumerated requests for relief, the Court finds as follows: (1) For the reasons set forth in plaintiff's opposition memorandum, there is no good cause shown on this record to compel surrender of plaintiff's cellphone for third-party examination. As the federal district court held in Memry Corp. v. Kentucky Oil Technology, N.V., 207 WL 832937 (N.D. Cal. 2007), hard drive (and presumably cellphone) production and forensic examination is warranted only when the computer content is intricately related to the basis for the litigation and not when the demanding party's justification is, essentially, to ensure the party's document production has been full and complete. This is the case here. Plaintiff's cellphone/computer use is tangential to the grounds for her complaint, and defendants' desire to plumb the depths of her communications (subject to unspecified guardrails) is simply not compelling, notwithstanding plaintiff's dribbling out document production over a period of months. In response to defendant's objection about citing this decision, the Court is only doing so with reference to its reasoning, not as binding precedent. This "prong" of defendants' demand for relief is DENIED. (2) Under controlling authority (Catalina Island Yacht Club v. Superior Court, 242 Cal.App.4th 1116, 1130 (2015)), plaintiff's privilege log is manifestly insufficient. It does not have to disclose the substance of attorney-client or marital privilege communications. But it does have to break down the categorical references now in the log to show author, recipient, date and enough of a subject matter description to allow an objective assessment of the appropriateness of the invocation of privilege. This "prong" of defendants' demand for relief is GRANTED (end of part 3 see part 4 tentative ruling) = (302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DANIELLE DI BARI VS. WOODLINE PARTNERS LP, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT WOODLINE PARTNERS LP MOTION TO COMPEL COMPLIANCE WITH THE COURTS FEBRUARY 2, 2024 DISCOVERY ORDER AND FOR SANCTIONS FOR FAILURE TO OBEY ORDER (PART 4 OF 5)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 19, 2024, line 6, DEFENDANT WOODLINE PARTNERS LP MOTION TO COMPEL COMPLIANCE WITH THE COURTS FEBRUARY 2, 2024 DISCOVERY ORDER AND FOR SANCTIONS FOR FAILURE TO OBEY ORDER (PART 4 OF 5) (3) From the record now before it, the Court has no idea what the problem may be with plaintiff's Instagram-related production. Defendants' reference to "industry standards" does not contain any substance. If this data production is not viewable or useful, defendants will have to spell it out much more concretely than in its pending motion. For that reason, this "prong" of defendants' motion is DENIED. (4) The plaintiff's husband is not a true third-party in this litigation. Nor does he have to be an author of any of the gigabytes of data produced after his deposition concluded for relevant questions to be directed to him based on one or more of those newly disclosed communications. Plaintiff offers no reason why the June 18 data dump could not have been made sufficiently in advance of both scheduled depositions to allow defense counsel to use it in their preparations. As for the need for a second subpoena and another round of meet and confer efforts (to what end, pray tell), in this context those demands are a bridge too far. This "prong" of defendants' motion is GRANTED. Defendants are entitled to another six hours of deposition of plaintiff's husband. (5) The record shows several months of ill-conducted discovery cooperation and performance by plaintiff. Her halting and incomplete document production, even if not deliberate, is simply unacceptable. Plaintiff has not shown substantial justification for her discovery abuses, entitling defendants to an award of sanctions. The Court, however, believes the labor defendants claim to have put into their efforts to enforce the February 2 order and get this motion prepared is over-stated. Accordingly, sanctions of $10,000 are GRANTED jointly against plaintiff and her counsel, payable within 15 days of the filing date of the order resolving this motion in its entirety. The Court will forego any sanctions payable to it. (6) As for the alternative relief of terminating (or issue or evidentiary) sanctions, "[S]anctions are generally imposed in an incremental approach, with terminating sanctions being the last resort." Dept. of Forestry & Fire Protection v. Howell, 18 Cal. App. 5th 154, 191 (2017). Where a violation is willful, preceded by a history of abuse, and the evidence shows that less severe sanctions would not produce compliance with the discovery rules, the trial court is justified in imposing the ultimate sanction. August B. Doppes v. Bentley Motors, Inc., 174 Cal.App.4th 967, 992 (2009). Here, however, although plaintiff's discovery performance has been frustrating, incomplete and haphazard, terminating sanctions are not warranted. Other sanctions in the form of evidentiary or issue preclusion are not sufficiently spelled out in defendants' pleadings to be considered. For those reasons, this "prong" of defendants' requested relief is DENIED. Defendants' counsel are directed to prepare a form of proposed order consistent with the foregoing tentative rulings, submit it to plaintiff's counsel in compliance with Rule 3.1312 of the Rules of Court, and then forward it to psw@hassard.com for review and signature. (end of part 4 seet part 5 tentative ruling) = (302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DANIELLE DI BARI VS. WOODLINE PARTNERS LP, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT WOODLINE PARTNERS LP MOTION TO COMPEL COMPLIANCE WITH THE COURTS FEBRUARY 2, 2024 DISCOVERY ORDER AND FOR SANCTIONS FOR FAILURE TO OBEY ORDER (PART 2 OF 5)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 19, 2024, line 6, DEFENDANT WOODLINE PARTNERS LP MOTION TO COMPEL COMPLIANCE WITH THE COURTS FEBRUARY 2, 2024 DISCOVERY ORDER AND FOR SANCTIONS FOR FAILURE TO OBEY ORDER (PART 2 OF 5) From and after February 2, 2024, plaintiff made a series of additional document productions (accompanied by a so-called "barebones" privilege log) from her cellphone and related sources, during the course of which plaintiff's counsel made a number of "that's it, there's nothing more" representations in meet and confer communications with defense counsel - reminiscent of a recent and rather notorious attorney declaration swearing that "all records have been returned" from a well-known Florida residence when, in fact, that proved not to be the case. The parties agreed on deposition dates for plaintiff (June 20 and 21) and her husband (June 18), with defense counsel emailing her need for any and all new document productions several days prior to the start of depositions. The husband's deposition took place on June 18. That evening, however, plaintiff served what is described as 2.6 GB (presumably that's a lot of paper) of additional documents responsive to various requests in Set No. 1. As a result of this production, plaintiff's deposition did not go forward. Currently before the Court is defendants' motion for an order compelling plaintiff's further compliance with the Court's February 2, 2024 order (i.e. compelling production of any remaining documents responsive to Set No.1's social media/email requests not already produced) and seeking additional relief by way of an order (1) compelling plaintiff to produce her cellphone for third-party forensic examination to ensure that all responsive documents have indeed been produced; (2) requiring submission of a new and adequate privilege log; (3) reproduction of certain Instagram videos in compliance with "industry standards"; (4) requiring plaintiff's husband to sit for a second deposition session; (5) awarding sanctions of $17,200 to the defendants and $1,000 to the court or, in the alternative, (6) terminating sanctions for plaintiff's allegedly repeated and intentional discovery abuses. Suffice it to say, plaintiff opposes each of defendants' requests for relief. She contends terminating sanctions are not warranted on the record before the Court, there is no good cause shown to compel her to produce her cellphone for forensic examination, her previous privilege log is code-compliant, the "industry standards" defendants reference are undefined and unnecessary, that absent a new round of meeting and conferring (and service of a second subpoena), the Court may not order plaintiff's husband to appear for a second deposition and defendants have not shown good cause for an award of additional sanctions, never mind an award of $17,200/$1,000 as prayed for in the pending motion. (end part 2, see part 3 tentative ruling) = (302/JPT)

rulings.Calendar Matter: PLAINTIFF MARSHALL HOLLAND , AN INDIVIDUAL NOTICE OF MOTION AND MEMORANDUM OF POINTS AND AUTHORITIES FILED IN SUPPORT OF HIS MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER DISCOVERY RESPONSES FROM DEFENDANT, THE MOSSER COMPANIES, INC. TO FORM INTERROGATORIES - EMPLOYMENT LAW, SETS ONE AND TWO(TENTATIVE RULING PART 3 OF 5)
rulings.Rulings: PLAINTIFF MARSHALL HOLLAND , AN INDIVIDUAL NOTICE OF MOTION AND MEMORANDUM OF POINTS AND AUTHORITIES FILED IN SUPPORT OF HIS MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER DISCOVERY RESPONSES FROM DEFENDANT, THE MOSSER COMPANIES, INC. TO FORM INTERROGATORIES - EMPLOYMENT LAW, SETS ONE AND TWO(TENTATIVE RULING PART 3 OF 5) Defendant must also provide unambiguous and direct responses to the numerous admission requests identified in plaintiff's motion which defendant more or less blew off for reasons that are unsupportable on this record. This finding and characterization of defendant's responses is particularly applicable to those admission requests addressing the federal government loan program (PPP) restrictions that are neither vague nor otherwise objectionable. Similarly, to the degree defendant may rely upon or invoke the idea of "financial distress" as a defense or justification for its actions herein, the requests addressed to that subject are entitled to direct and factual responses. And if a response is to deny the request, the Court directs defendant's attention to those interrogatories calling for factual, documentary and witness support for such denials, none of which have been satisfactorily addressed in defendant's responses. Plaintiff's motion directed to the Request for Admissions is GRANTED. When it comes to the subject of defendant's financial privacy, if indeed the defendant has alluded to or relied upon that ground in its responses, the Court also directs defendant's attention to the dispositive holding in SCC Acquisitions, Inc. v. Superior Court, 243 Cal.App.4th 741, 755-756 (2015) for the principle that companies have limited privacy interests that need to be balanced against whether the sought-after information is reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. The Court does not find that any of plaintiff's discovery touching upon defendant's finances amounts to an objectionable invasion of its privacy interests, at least not on the basis of the record now before it. At the risk of repeating itself, the Court also finds that defendant's responses to plaintiff's special interrogatories are inadequate, not code-compliant and significantly evasive and dilatory. For those reasons, plaintiff's motion to compel further responses is GRANTED. Defendant's responses to plaintiff's Request for Production of Documents also fall well short of its obligations under the Discovery Act. For many of the same reasons addressed above, defendant's boilerplate objections are not well taken and amount to an abuse of the discovery process. Are there ESI parameters that need to be discussed? If so, the record shows no effort by defendant to spell out those parameters or explain why responding to specific document requests would be hamstrung by ESI complications. Plaintiff's motion to compel directed to defendant's responses to the Request for Production of Documents is GRANTED. (end of part 3 see part 4 tentative ruling) = (302/JPT)

rulings.Calendar Matter: PLAINTIFF MARSHALL HOLLAND , AN INDIVIDUAL NOTICE OF MOTION AND MEMORANDUM OF POINTS AND AUTHORITIES FILED IN SUPPORT OF HIS MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER DISCOVERY RESPONSES FROM DEFENDANT, THE MOSSER COMPANIES, INC. TO FORM INTERROGATORIES - EMPLOYMENT LAW, SETS ONE AND TWO(TENTATIVE RULING PART 4 OF 5)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 19, 2024, line 7, PLAINTIFF MARSHALL HOLLAND , AN INDIVIDUAL NOTICE OF MOTION AND MEMORANDUM OF POINTS AND AUTHORITIES FILED IN SUPPORT OF HIS MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER DISCOVERY RESPONSES FROM DEFENDANT, THE MOSSER COMPANIES, INC. TO FORM INTERROGATORIES - EMPLOYMENT LAW, SETS ONE AND TWO(TENTATIVE RULING PART 4 OF 5) Finally, in an "omnibus" opposition pleading filed on July 8, 2024, defendant argues that the four motions to compel are moot, unnecessary and premature because, as of that date, defendant has now supplemented its discovery responses to eliminate (or narrow) the discovery disputes between the parties. These supplementary responses (which are not part of the record before the Court) are long past due, having been promised by the several defense counsel handling this file for literally months. Plaintiff had good cause to finally pull the trigger by resorting to the Court for assistance in receiving the discovery responses to which he had been entitled over the extended period of meet and confer communications (or lack thereof on defendant's part). The plaintiff's motions are not moot, and certainly were not premature and if defendant's supplementary responses do reduce the number of insufficiencies in its discovery responses, that remains to be determined by the parties and, potentially, in further motion proceedings. On the subject of sanctions, plaintiff has satisfactorily outlined the governing principles in each of the four pending motions, citing among others to Code of Civil Procedure sections 2023.010 and 2031.300(c). The Court finds that the record shows no substantial justifications for defendant's discovery missteps or other circumstances that would make an award of sanctions unjust. The amount of effort put into resolving these discovery disputes by plaintiff's counsel is well supported in the record before the Court. The hours and rates quoted are certainly reasonable and, likely, understated. Accordingly, the Court awards plaintiff sanctions in the amount of $17,300 as against defendant and its counsel, jointly and severally, payable within 15 days of the date of this Court's order. The Court directs counsel for plaintiff to prepare a proposed order which, in sufficient detail, recites the multitude of interrogatories, admission requests, special interrogatories and document requests which are the subject of these four motions to compel and to which further responses are due and owing. That order should also include the sanctions award made by the Court. The fact that defendant has now supplemented its discovery responses, to whatever degree it has, is irrelevant to and need not be taken into account in the drafting of this proposed order. The Court agrees with defendant that further meet and confer communications between counsel should be able to reduce if not eliminate any remaining discovery disputes, but that representation comes "a whole lot of days late and a lot of dollars short." (end of part 4 see part 5 tentative ruling) = (302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MARSHALL HOLLAND VS. THE MOSSER COMPANIES, INC., A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: PLAINTIFF MARSHALL HOLLAND , AN INDIVIDUAL NOTICE OF MOTION AND MEMORANDUM OF POINTS AND AUTHORITIES FILED IN SUPPORT OF HIS MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER DISCOVERY RESPONSES FROM DEFENDANT, THE MOSSER COMPANIES, INC. TO FORM INTERROGATORIES - EMPLOYMENT LAW, SETS ONE AND TWO(TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 5)
rulings.Rulings: PLAINTIFF MARSHALL HOLLAND , AN INDIVIDUAL NOTICE OF MOTION AND MEMORANDUM OF POINTS AND AUTHORITIES FILED IN SUPPORT OF HIS MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER DISCOVERY RESPONSES FROM DEFENDANT, THE MOSSER COMPANIES, INC. TO FORM INTERROGATORIES - EMPLOYMENT LAW, SETS ONE AND TWO(TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 5) As a general proposition, one particularly applicable to the pending motions, the scope of discovery is quite broad permitting discovery regarding any matter that is relevant to the subject matter. (CCP 2017.010.) The "subject matter" is broader than issues framed in the pleadings and includes potential issues in the case. Pacific Tel. & Tel. Co. v. Superior Court, 2 Cal.3d 161, 172-173 (1970). For purposes of discovery, information is considered relevant if it might reasonably assist a party in evaluating the case, preparing for trial, or facilitating settlement thereof. Lipton v. Superior Court, 48 Cal.App.4th 1599, 1611 (1996); Norton v. Superior Court (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 1750, 1760. The party seeking discovery is entitled to substantial leeway and doubts are resolved in favor of allowing discovery. (Norton, at 1761.). The four motions to compel address literally scores of questions, admission requests and requests for production of documents propounded to defendant. The Court does not intend to address the merits of defendant's responses to each and every one. Suffice it to say, the Court finds that in virtually every circumstance addressed by plaintiff in his four motions, the defendant's responses were inadequate under the governing provisions of the Code of Civil Procedure. Many of defendant's responses consisted of boilerplate objections rattled off in extended paragraphs with no substantive content. Objections based on vagueness, overbreadth, privilege, third-party privacy, conclusions of fact/law and the burdensome nature of the inquiries are meritless and seemingly offered without a good faith basis in fact or law. Those objections, therefore, are overruled. Once the vast majority of defendant's objections are set aside, it is clear that defendant's responses to plaintiff's employment form interrogatories must be supplemented with direct and factual responses and without further objections. If there is any legitimate basis for invoking a privilege objection, the defendant must do so by submitting a privilege log with sufficient detail to allow meaningful review of its merits. See Catalina Island Yacht Club v. Superior Court, 242 Cal.App.4th 1116, 1130 (2015). This is particularly the case when it comes to attorney-client and work product assertions. Questions asking defendant to identify those interviewed about the merits of plaintiff's claims, or from whom statements have been taken, do not warrant privilege objections. The motion directed to defendant's responses to the two sets of employment form interrogatories is GRANTED. (end of part 2 see part 3 tentative ruling) = (302/JPT)

If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff has brought a wrongful termination action against defendant, his former employer. The gist of the plaintiff's several causes of action is that defendant engaged in numerous violations of the Fair Employment and Housing Act (FEHA), Government Code sections 12900, et seq., with respect to a failure to accommodate plaintiff's mental disabilities. According to plaintiff's complaint, he had worked for defendant for almost five years while receiving various accommodations including remote work, noise-cancelling headphones, office barriers to eliminate distractions and the like. Then, with a new human resources director in place, plaintiff was denied appropriate accommodations going forward and terminated. Plaintiff also alleges that defendant received federal government financial assistance during the COVID pandemic that came with layoff restrictions that his termination violated. Plaintiff propounded several forms of written discovery to defendant: two sets of employment practice form interrogatories, a Request for Admissions, two sets of special interrogatories and a Request for Production of Documents. In four motions to compel further responses, one for each form of discovery, plaintiff complains that defendant's responses are not code-compliant, are evasive and manifestly inadequate on a host of grounds. Working through a revolving cast of defense counsel, plaintiff's counsel engaged in several months of meet and confer efforts in order to secure sufficient responses to the propounded discovery, but to little or no avail. As a result, plaintiff filed the discovery motions now pending before the Court, In addition to an order (or four orders) compelling further responses/answers to the discovery, plaintiff also requests an award of sanctions for repeated discovery abuses by defendant in the amount of $17,730, representing the time and effort plaintiff's counsel expended in informal attempts to secure further discovery responses and finally to draft, support and argue the four discovery motions. (end of part 1 see part 2 tentative ruling) = (302/JPT)

The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(302/RCE)
